response: Let $g_{a,b} (a,b)$ denote $f(a,b)$. Thus, the equations can be written similarly: \begin{align*}  g_{aa} &= a, \\  g_{ab} &= g_{ba},  \quad \text{and} \\ (a + b) g_{ab} &= bg_{a,a+b}.  \end{align*} Thus, $$(a + b)(a + b) g_{ab} = b b g_{a,a+b},$$ or $$g_{ab} = \tfrac{b}{a+b} g_{a,a+b}.$$ This is an interesting reduction of two variables, and we can use this to our advantage. Writing $g_{a,c}$ for $g_{a,a+c}$, we have \begin{align*}  g_{ab} &= \tfrac{b}{a+b} g_{a,a+b} \\  &= \tfrac{b}{a+b} , \cdot \tfrac{a+c}{a+c} g_{a+c,a+b+c} \\  &= \tfrac{b}{a+b} \cdot \tfrac{a+c}{a+c} g_{a+c,a+b+c} \\  &= \tfrac{b}{a+b} \cdot \tfrac{a+c}{a+c} \cdot \tfrac{a+2c}{a+2c} g_{a+2c,a+b+2c} \\  &\quad \vdots \\  &= \prod_{k=0}^{b-1} \tfrac{a+kc}{a+kc} g_{a+bc,a+bk} = \prod_{k=0}^{b-1} \tfrac{a+kc}{a+kc} \cdot b, \end{align*} where the reduction is buried in the product which is geometric. The formula for $g_{a,b}$ is therefore \[ g_{a,b} = \frac{ab}{a+b} \cdot \prod_{k=0}^{b-1} \tfrac{a+kc}{a+kc}. \]  Plugging in $(a,b) = (14,52)$, only the product remains. The pattern in the numerator and denominator suggests to do \end{align*} <answer> $\tfrac{52}{6} \times 7 \times 8 \times 9 \times 10 \times 11 \times 12 \times 13 = 245,044,800$ </answer>
